mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the push operation in a stack implemented using a linked list?
mcqs.explanation: The push operation in a linked list stack implementation adds a new node at the beginning (head), which is a constant time operation O(1) requiring no traversal. We simply create a new node, set its next pointer to the current top, and update the top pointer to the new node.

mcqs.question: In a queue implemented using a linked list, which pointers are typically maintained?
mcqs.explanation: A linked list queue implementation maintains both front and rear pointers to enable efficient O(1) enqueue (add at rear) and dequeue (remove from front) operations. If only one pointer were used, either enqueue or dequeue would require O(n) traversal to find the other end.

mcqs.question: What happens when you attempt to pop from an empty linked stack (with proper error handling)?

options.text: Returns a special error value or reports underflow
Creates a new node with default value

mcqs.explanation: Proper implementation should check for an empty stack (top == NULL) and return a special error value (like INT_MIN in C) or print an underflow message to handle the condition gracefully, preventing crashes and informing the caller of the error.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a key advantage of linked list implementation over array implementation for stacks and queues?

options.text: No extra memory overhead per element
Dynamic size — no overflow unless memory is exhausted

mcqs.explanation: Linked list implementation provides dynamic size flexibility, allowing the data structure to grow and shrink as needed without a predefined maximum size, unlike fixed-size arrays that suffer from overflow when capacity is reached.

mcqs.question: In a linked stack, where are new elements (push) added?

options.text: At the end of the list
At the beginning of the list (head/top)
In the middle of the list

mcqs.explanation: New elements are added at the beginning (head/top) of the linked list to maintain O(1) time complexity for push operations. Adding at the head requires only pointer updates without any traversal, and this location naturally represents the 'top' of the stack.

mcqs.question: What is the primary disadvantage of using linked lists for stacks and queues compared to arrays?

options.text: Extra memory overhead for storing pointers in each node
Linked lists have a fixed maximum size

mcqs.explanation: Each node in a linked list requires additional memory to store the pointer (next field) alongside the data. On a 64-bit system, each node has 8 bytes of pointer overhead. Arrays store only the data elements contiguously with no per-element pointer overhead.

mcqs.question: In a linked queue implementation, after a dequeue operation when the front becomes NULL, what should happen to the rear pointer?

options.text: Rear pointer should also be set to NULL
Rear pointer should point to garbage

mcqs.explanation: When the last element is dequeued, front becomes NULL. In this case, rear must also be set to NULL to maintain consistency. Otherwise, rear would point to freed memory, leading to dangling pointer issues and incorrect empty queue detection in future operations.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the 'free()' or 'delete' operation in stack pop or queue dequeue?

options.text: To increase the size of the stack/queue
To prevent memory leaks by releasing heap memory
To make the next element accessible
To initialize the pointers to NULL

mcqs.explanation: The free() operation is critical for preventing memory leaks. When we remove a node from a linked list, we must free the heap memory allocated to that node. Forgetting to free causes the memory to remain allocated even though it's no longer accessible, leading to memory leaks that can exhaust system memory over time.
